---
title: "Session 4: From Data to Deliverable"
subtitle: "Business Intelligence & AI"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
---

## Homework Debrief {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the homework debrief (minutes 0-10). Students share their best and worst answers from the XYZ Corp exploration homework. The goal is to surface common failure patterns and connect them to the maker/checker framework from Session 2.
:::

---

## Best and Worst Answers

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Share Your Best Answer
:::
- What question did you ask?
- What made the answer good?
- Would you trust it without further checking?
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Share Your Worst Answer
:::
- What went wrong?
- Was the answer wrong, misleading, or just incomplete?
- What verification would have caught it?
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Before we dive into new material, let's debrief the homework. You spent this week asking the XYZ Corp Data Simulation at least 10 business questions. I want to hear about your best answer and your worst answer."

Call on 3-4 students. For each, ask: "What question did you ask? What made the answer good --- or bad?" Listen for common patterns:
- Good answers: clear data, well-structured tables, accurate calculations
- Bad answers: wrong aggregations, missing filters, misleading charts, hallucinated context

After sharing, connect to Session 2: "Remember the maker/checker framework? Every one of these answers needs a different level of verification. A chart for your own understanding? Quick glance. A number going into a board presentation? Verify the SQL, check the filters, confirm the row counts."

Transition: "Now let me show you what happens when we go from single questions to complete deliverables."
:::

---

## Applying Maker/Checker

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Low Stakes
:::
- Internal exploration, brainstorming
- "How many customers does each division have?"
- Quick sanity check: do the numbers look reasonable?
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Medium Stakes
:::
- Team presentations, internal reports
- "Revenue per employee by division"
- Verify: check the SQL, confirm date ranges, spot-check totals
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
High Stakes
:::
- Board decks, regulatory filings
- "Quarterly executive summary for the CEO"
- [Independent verification of every claim]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "The maker/checker framework from Session 2 applies directly to enterprise data. The stakes determine the level of checking. For internal exploration, a quick sanity check is fine. For a board presentation, you verify every number independently."

"Today we are going to produce high-stakes deliverables --- the kind of documents that actually get distributed and acted on. That means the verification skills you learned in Session 2 are essential."

Transition: "Let me show you the full pipeline."
:::

---

## Full Pipeline Demo {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the full pipeline demo (minutes 10-25). This is the showpiece demo of Session 4. Make sure the XYZ Corp Data Simulation is open and ready. You will ask for a quarterly executive summary and then iterate on it.
:::

---

## From Questions to Deliverables

::: {.explainer}
In Sessions 1--3, we asked questions and got answers. Today we chain those steps into **complete reporting pipelines** --- from raw data to polished, board-ready documents.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
One prompt. Six systems queried. Charts generated. Narrative written. Document assembled. **That is the pipeline.**
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "In Sessions 1 through 3, we asked questions and got answers. A number. A chart. A paragraph of explanation. Useful, but not a deliverable. Today we chain those steps into complete reporting pipelines --- from raw data to polished, board-ready documents."

"Here is what that looks like: one prompt. Six systems queried. Charts generated. Narrative written. Document assembled. That is the pipeline."

"This is where AI data agents go from 'interesting tool' to 'transformative capability.' Answering questions saves minutes. Automating reporting pipelines saves days."
:::

---

## The Pipeline Steps

::: {.step-flow-6}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1
:::
::: {.step-title}
Query
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Pull from 6 source systems
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2
:::
::: {.step-title}
Merge
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Join and deduplicate
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3
:::
::: {.step-title}
Compute
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Calculate KPIs and variances
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4
:::
::: {.step-title}
Chart
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Generate visualizations
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
5
:::
::: {.step-title}
Narrate
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Write analysis text
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
6
:::
::: {.step-title}
Assemble
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Format final document
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through each step before switching to the live demo. Say: "Here is what is about to happen. I am going to ask the XYZ Corp Data Simulation one question: 'Prepare a quarterly executive summary.' Watch what the agent does."

"Step one: it will query six source systems --- the three CRMs, the financial systems, Workday HR, and Zendesk support. Step two: merge and deduplicate. Step three: compute KPIs --- quarter-over-quarter changes, efficiency ratios, concentration metrics. Step four: charts. Step five: narrative analysis --- not just numbers, but interpretation. Step six: assemble everything into a structured document."

"Let me show you."

Switch to the Data Simulation.
:::

---

## Live Demo: "Prepare a Quarterly Executive Summary" {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
The prompt
:::
```
Prepare a quarterly executive summary of XYZ Corp performance ---
revenue by division, headcount efficiency, top customer risks, and
supply chain issues. Include charts and a narrative summary.
```
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the Data Simulation. Type the prompt. As it runs, narrate what you see: "It is starting with the CRM queries --- pulling revenue by division from each system. Now Workday for headcount. Now Zendesk for support tickets. SAP and Oracle for supply chain."

Let the full result render. It should produce:
- Revenue by division table with QoQ comparison
- Headcount efficiency metrics
- Customer concentration risk flags
- Supply chain issue summary
- Charts and narrative text

Point out the key insight: "This is a complete executive summary. Revenue, headcount, customer risk, supply chain. All from one prompt. Six systems queried. Charts generated. Narrative written."

If the demo fails, switch to the fallback recording and narrate over it.
:::

---

## Demo Result: Q4 Executive Summary {.shrink}

::: {.comparison-table}

| Division | Q4 2025 | Q3 2025 | QoQ Growth | Active Customers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial | $5,891,237 | $5,482,103 | +7.5% | 91 |
| Energy | $3,412,891 | $3,187,445 | +7.1% | 65 |
| Safety | $1,147,892 | $1,023,556 | +12.1% | 37 |
| **Total** | **$10,452,020** | **$9,693,104** | **+7.8%** | **193** |

:::

::: {.highlight-box}
Safety posted the strongest growth rate at **12.1%**. But Energy has a **34% customer concentration risk** in CAT Energy Services --- a single-point-of-failure.
:::

::: {.notes}
Display this slide alongside or after the demo result. Say: "Here is the revenue breakdown. All three divisions grew quarter over quarter. Safety had the strongest growth rate at 12.1 percent, though from a smaller base. Total revenue: $10.5 million, up 7.8 percent."

"But the agent also flagged a risk: Energy division has 34 percent customer concentration in CAT Energy Services. If that one customer leaves, Energy loses one-third of its revenue. No dashboard would have surfaced that unprompted. The agent did because the prompt asked for 'top customer risks.'"
:::

---

## Now Iterate

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Draft 1: Data Summary
:::
- Revenue by division, headcount, growth rates
- Comprehensive but flat
- "Summarize Q4 performance"
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Draft 2: Traffic-Light Format
:::
- Red / Yellow / Green status by division
- Flags anything >10% off plan
- "Traffic-light format, flag >10% off plan"
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Draft 3: Executive Brief
:::
- Leads with the biggest risk
- Ends with 3 recommended actions
- ["One page, lead with risk, end with actions"]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.case-quote}
Same data. Same agent. [The prompt was the variable that mattered.]{.amber}
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: You can either do this live or show the prepared results. If live, iterate on the summary:
1. The first output was a comprehensive data dump
2. Now ask: "Reformat this as a traffic-light dashboard --- red, yellow, green --- flag anything more than 10 percent off plan"
3. Then: "Make this one page. Lead with the biggest risk. End with three recommended actions."

Walk through the three drafts: "Same data. Same agent. The prompt was the variable that mattered. Draft 1 was a data dump --- technically correct but not useful to an executive. Draft 2 added structure --- the traffic-light format makes it scannable. Draft 3 leads with risk and ends with actions --- that is what a board wants to see."

"This is the iteration skill from Session 2, applied to enterprise deliverables. The first draft is rarely the best draft. The skill is knowing how to refine."
:::

---

## Three Iterations Compared {.shrink}

::: {.comparison-table}

| Draft | Prompt Addition | Output | Audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "Summarize Q4 performance" | Data dump --- no prioritization | Analyst |
| 2 | + "Traffic-light, flag >10% off plan" | Structured dashboard --- scannable | Manager |
| 3 | + "One page, lead with risk, end with actions" | Executive brief --- board-ready | C-suite |

:::

::: {.explainer}
The prompt is not just a question --- it is a **specification**. More specific prompts produce more useful deliverables. This is the skill you developed in Session 2, now applied to enterprise data.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Let me make the pattern explicit. The prompt is not just a question. It is a specification. Each iteration added specificity: format, thresholds, priorities, audience. And each iteration produced a more useful output."

"This is the skill you developed in Session 2 --- iteration, critique, refinement --- now applied to enterprise data. The maker/checker framework still applies. But now you are checking not just accuracy, but whether the output serves its intended audience."

Transition: "Now let me show you what happens behind the scenes."
:::

---

## How the Agent Loop Works {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the agent loop explanation (minutes 25-38). You are pulling back the curtain on the technical architecture. The goal is not to make students into developers, but to give them enough understanding to have informed conversations with technical teams and vendors.
:::

---

## The Core Loop

::: {.step-flow}
::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
1
:::
::: {.step-title}
System Prompt
:::
::: {.step-desc}
Schema description: what tables, columns, and relationships exist
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
2
:::
::: {.step-title}
Tool Call
:::
::: {.step-desc}
LLM writes SQL and requests execution
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
3
:::
::: {.step-title}
Execute & Return
:::
::: {.step-desc}
System runs SQL, sends results back to LLM
:::
:::

::: {.step-card}
::: {.step-icon}
4
:::
::: {.step-title}
Test & Review
:::
::: {.step-desc}
LLM checks results --- correct? try again? deliver?
:::
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
This is a **test-and-review cycle**, not a one-shot generation. The LLM writes code, the system executes it, the LLM reviews the results, and decides: deliver or try again.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Let me pull back the curtain. When you ask the Data Simulation a question, four things happen."

Walk through each step: "Step one: the system prompt. Before your question ever reaches the LLM, a system prompt has been loaded that describes every table, every column, and every relationship. This is the institutional knowledge layer --- it tells the LLM what data exists and what it means."

"Step two: the tool call. The LLM reads your question, reads the schema, and writes SQL. But it does not just return the SQL. It requests execution --- it says 'run this query against this database.'"

"Step three: the system runs the SQL and sends the results back to the LLM. The LLM never touches the database directly."

"Step four --- and this is the key insight: the LLM reviews the results. If the result set is empty, or the numbers look wrong, or an error occurred, it tries again. It rewrites the SQL, changes a filter, fixes a join. This is a test-and-review cycle, not a one-shot generation."
:::

---

## The System Prompt Is Everything {.shrink}

::: {.code-box}
::: {.box-title}
What the LLM sees before your question
:::
```
You are a data analyst for XYZ Corp, a $500M B2B distributor.

Available databases:
- sf_opportunities: Salesforce CRM (Industrial division)
  Columns: OpportunityId, AccountName, Amount, CloseDate, Stage...
- legacy_orders: Legacy CRM (Energy division)
  Columns: order_id, customer_name, order_amount, order_date (VARCHAR!)...
- hs_deals: HubSpot CRM (Safety division)
  Columns: deal_id, company_name, amount, close_date...
- wd_employees: Workday HR
  Columns: employee_id, division, department, hire_date, salary...

IMPORTANT: legacy_orders stores dates as MM/DD/YYYY text strings.
IMPORTANT: Customer names differ across systems. Use fuzzy matching.
```
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "This is a simplified version of what the LLM sees before your question arrives. The system prompt describes every table, every column, and every gotcha. Look at the notes at the bottom: the Legacy CRM stores dates as text strings, not proper dates. Customer names differ across systems --- use fuzzy matching."

"This system prompt is the institutional knowledge layer. If the agent gets a query wrong, the fix is often not in the code --- it is in the schema description. Change the description, change the SQL."

"Who writes this? In a production deployment, it is a combination of data engineers and business analysts. Someone who knows both the technical schema and what the business actually means when it says 'revenue' or 'active customer.'"
:::

---

## The Test-and-Review Cycle

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
What Makes This Different from a Dashboard
:::
- Dashboards run **pre-written** queries
- If the query is wrong, you get wrong answers forever
- Changing the question means changing the code
- No self-correction capability
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
What the Agent Does
:::
- Writes a **new query** for every question
- Reviews results: empty set? error? retry
- Can rewrite SQL 2-3 times before delivering
- [Self-corrects --- but not infallibly]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "This test-and-review cycle is what makes agents fundamentally different from dashboards. A dashboard runs pre-written queries. If the query has a bug, you get wrong answers until someone fixes the code. The agent writes a new query for every question, reviews the results, and can self-correct."

"But --- and this is important --- self-correction is not infallible. The agent can retry 2 or 3 times and still get it wrong. It might think an empty result set means 'no data' when the real problem is a wrong filter. That is why the maker/checker framework matters. The agent corrects many of its own errors. You catch the rest."
:::

---

## From Ad Hoc to Automated {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the "ad hoc to automated" segment (minutes 38-48). You are bridging from interactive Q&A to scheduled reporting pipelines. The HPE case study provides real-world validation.
:::

---

## The Production Pattern

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
What You Have Been Doing
:::
- Type a question in a chat interface
- Get an answer in real time
- Useful --- but manual and ad hoc
- You still have to remember to ask
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
What Production Looks Like
:::
- Same agent loop, triggered by a **schedule**
- Monday morning dashboard refresh
- Monthly board deck, weekly sales forecast
- [Error handling + version control + human review gates]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Everything you have done so far has been interactive. You type a question, you get an answer. That is useful. But in production, these pipelines run on schedules."

"Imagine the quarterly executive summary you just saw, but it runs automatically on the first Monday of every quarter. It queries the same six systems, generates the same charts, writes the same narrative. But instead of you typing the prompt, a cron job triggers it."

"What changes in production? Three things: error handling --- what happens when a database is down or a query returns unexpected results? Version control --- can you reproduce last month's report exactly? And human review gates --- the report is drafted but held for approval before distribution."
:::

---

## Real-World Benchmark: HPE {.quote-slide}

## "Alfred" --- HPE's AI Agent

::: {.quote-text}
HPE's CFO Marie Myers deployed an AI agent that replaced a [100-slide weekly performance review]{.amber} that consumed hundreds of person-hours. The agent queries financial systems, generates analysis, and produces executive summaries --- the same pipeline you just saw.
:::

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
40%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
faster reporting cycle
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
25%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
lower processing costs
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
90%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
reduction in manual prep
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "This is not hypothetical. HPE's CFO Marie Myers deployed an AI agent internally called Alfred. It replaced a 100-slide weekly performance review that consumed hundreds of person-hours of manual preparation."

"The results: 40 percent faster reporting cycle. 25 percent lower processing costs. 90 percent reduction in manual prep. This is exactly the pipeline you just saw --- querying enterprise systems, generating analysis, producing executive summaries --- running at scale in a Fortune 500 company."

"This is the benchmark. When you think about your capstone strategy and what is realistic, HPE shows that automated reporting pipelines are not a future promise --- they are a current reality."
:::

---

## Live Walkthrough: Building an Agent {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the live build segment (minutes 48-53). The instructor builds a simple agent on the AI+Code Lab using Claude Code. This is a quick, high-impact demonstration --- 5 minutes, not a full coding session. The goal is the "your developer could do this" moment.
:::

---

## From English to Working Code

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
What You Are About to See
:::
- The instructor opens a terminal on the AI+Code Lab
- Types English instructions to Claude Code: "Build a Python script that takes a question, sends it to Claude with a schema description, gets SQL back, runs it, and prints the answer"
- Claude Code generates ~50 lines of Python
- We run it. We ask a question. We get an answer.
:::

::: {.highlight-box}
The entire agent --- from blank file to working code --- takes about **5 minutes**. This is the "your developer could do this" moment.
:::

::: {.notes}
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the AI+Code Lab terminal. Open an empty file. Tell Claude Code in English to build the basic agent. Narrate as it generates code:

"Watch --- I am telling Claude Code in plain English to build a data agent. 'Build a Python script that takes a question, sends it to Claude with a schema description, gets back SQL, runs it against a parquet file, and prints the answer.'"

"It is writing the code now. About 50 lines. There is the schema description. There is the tool call mechanism. There is the execution loop."

Run it. Ask: "How many employees does each division have?" Show the answer.

Say: "That is the entire core agent. Fifty lines of code. Built from English instructions in about 5 minutes. The XYZ Corp Data Simulation you have been using is built on this same core loop --- it just has 3,000 more lines for error handling, multiple databases, authentication, and the web interface."

"The gap between 50 lines and 3,000 lines is the gap between prototype and production. That gap is real. But the core is simple."

If the live demo fails, describe what would have happened and show a pre-prepared screenshot of the code.
:::

---

## The 50 Lines vs. the 3,000 Lines

::: {.two-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
50 Lines: The Prototype
:::
- System prompt with schema
- Tool call mechanism (SQL execution)
- Basic response formatting
- Works for one database, one user
- **This is what you just saw**
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
3,000 Lines: Production
:::
- Multiple database connections
- Authentication and access control
- Error handling and retry logic
- Logging, monitoring, audit trail
- Web interface, API, scheduling
- [This is the XYZ Corp Data Simulation]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
The core is simple. Production is engineering. Understanding the difference helps you evaluate vendors and scope internal builds.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "The core agent is 50 lines. The production application is 3,000 lines. What is the other 2,950 lines? Multiple database connections. Authentication. Error handling. Logging. Monitoring. A web interface. Scheduling. All the things that make it reliable, secure, and usable by more than one person."

"Why does this matter to you? Two reasons. First, when a vendor shows you a demo, you now know that the demo is the easy part. The hard part is the production engineering. Second, when someone on your team says 'I can build this,' they are right about the 50-line prototype --- but make sure they are scoping the 3,000-line production version."
:::

---

## Hands-On: Build Your Own Report {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the hands-on segment (minutes 53-68). Students use the Data Simulation to produce specific deliverables. Three guided exercises, each requiring different combinations of data sources.
:::

---

## Three Report Exercises

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Exercise 1: Customer Risk Report
:::
- Combine CRM pipeline data with support ticket trends
- Which customers are high-revenue AND high-complaint?
- Include a chart and a recommendation
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Exercise 2: Division P&L Comparison
:::
- Revenue by division from CRMs
- Headcount from Workday
- Revenue per employee with commentary
- Compare divisions head-to-head
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
Exercise 3: Board Memo
:::
- Q3 vs Q4 performance across all divisions
- Charts, narrative, risk flags
- [Iterate: make it one page, lead with risk]{.amber}
- Use the Session 2 techniques
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Your turn. Three exercises, each producing a different type of deliverable. You do not need to do all three --- pick the one that interests you most and do it well. If you finish early, try another."

"Exercise 1: customer risk report. Combine CRM data with Zendesk support tickets. Which customers are both high-revenue and high-complaint? That is a risk signal."

"Exercise 2: division P&L comparison. Pull revenue from the CRMs and headcount from Workday. Calculate revenue per employee. Compare divisions."

"Exercise 3: the full board memo. Q3 vs Q4 performance across all divisions, with charts, narrative, and risk flags. Then iterate --- make it one page, lead with the biggest risk. Use the iteration techniques from Session 2."

"You have 15 minutes. Focus on one exercise and make it great. Iterate on your prompt --- the first draft is never the best draft."

Circulate and help. Watch for students who are struggling with prompt specificity --- coach them to be more specific about format, audience, and what to include.
:::

---

## Iteration Tips

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Getting Better Output
:::
- **Be specific about format:** "Traffic-light dashboard" vs. "one-page executive brief"
- **Name the audience:** "For the CFO" produces different output than "for the sales team"
- **Set thresholds:** "Flag anything more than 10% off plan"
- **Ask for critique:** "What is weak about this analysis? What would a skeptical CFO push back on?"
- **Iterate:** "Lead with risk. End with three actions. Cut to one page."
:::

::: {.notes}
Display this slide as a reference while students work. You do not need to walk through each point unless someone asks. The key coaching move is to watch for students getting mediocre output and saying "good enough" --- push them to iterate at least once.
:::

---

## Workshop: Your Reporting Candidates {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
This opens the workshop segment (minutes 68-80). Students shift from the XYZ Corp simulation to their own companies. They are identifying recurring reports that follow the data-to-deliverable pattern and could be candidates for automation.
:::

---

## Identify Your Pipelines

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Individual Exercise (12 minutes)
:::
For **2-3 recurring reports** at your company:

1. **What is the report?** (weekly status, monthly board deck, quarterly review, etc.)
2. **What systems feed it?** (CRM, ERP, HR, finance)
3. **Who builds it today?** How long does it take?
4. **What would automation look like?** Agent queries sources, generates draft, human reviews
:::

::: {.comparison-table}

| Report | Systems | Owner Today | Time | Automation Potential |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| *e.g., Weekly sales forecast* | *Salesforce + NetSuite* | *Sales Ops analyst* | *4 hours/week* | *High --- structured data, repeating format* |
| *e.g., Monthly board deck* | *All systems* | *FP&A team* | *3 days/month* | *Medium --- needs heavy human review* |
| | | | | |

:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Now think about your own company. What recurring reports follow the pattern we just saw --- data from enterprise systems, combined and formatted into a deliverable? I want you to identify 2 to 3 candidates."

"For each one: what is the report? What systems feed it? Who builds it today and how long does it take? And what would automation look like?"

"Be realistic about automation potential. A weekly sales forecast from structured CRM data is a strong candidate. A monthly board deck that requires significant judgment and interpretation is a weaker candidate --- or at least, the automation handles the data assembly and the human handles the analysis."

"This exercise connects directly to your capstone. The reports you identify here may become the use case you present in Session 8."

Give students the full 12 minutes. Circulate and help.
:::

---

## Where Does the Code Run? {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Brief preview segment (minutes 80-85). This sets up Session 5 on deployment architecture. Keep it high-level --- 5 minutes, not a deep dive.
:::

---

## Three Execution Options

::: {.three-cards}
::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Cloud LLM Sandbox
:::
- Code runs on the vendor's servers
- Easiest to set up
- Your data leaves your network
- What we use for the XYZ Corp demo (synthetic data)
:::

::: {.card .card-light}
::: {.card-title}
Your Own Containers
:::
- Code runs on your servers
- Data stays on-premise
- You manage infrastructure
- Higher control, higher cost
:::

::: {.card .card-dark}
::: {.card-title}
SQL-Only Mode
:::
- LLM only generates SQL, never sees raw data
- Your system executes the SQL locally
- Minimum data exposure
- [Most restrictive but most secure]{.amber}
:::
:::

::: {.explainer}
Our demo uses synthetic data in the cloud. Production deployments keep data on-premise. Session 5 digs into these tradeoffs in detail.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Quick preview. When you deploy an agent in production, where does the code actually run? Three options."

Walk through briefly: "Option one: the vendor's cloud. Easiest to set up, but your data leaves your network. That is what we use for the XYZ Corp demo --- the data is synthetic, so it does not matter. Option two: your own containers. Data stays on-premise. You manage everything. Higher control, higher cost. Option three: SQL-only mode. The LLM generates SQL but never sees the raw data. Your system executes the query locally and only sends aggregate results to the LLM."

"Each option has different security, cost, and capability tradeoffs. Session 5 is entirely about this decision. The pre-readings I am assigning tonight will prepare you for that discussion."
:::

---

## Homework {.section-divider}

::: {.notes}
Wrap-up segment (minutes 85-90).
:::

---

## This Week

::: {.info-box}
::: {.box-title}
Homework for Session 5
:::
1. **Produce one complete deliverable** using the XYZ Corp Data Simulation --- a memo, comparison, or risk assessment. Save or screenshot the output.
2. **Pre-reading (~35 min):**
    - Morgan Stanley case study (provided) --- a regulated firm's deployment decision
    - [AIMultiple: "Cloud LLM vs Local LLMs"](https://aimultiple.com/cloud-llm) --- three deployment options
    - "What Does Your AI Actually See?" (provided) --- four data security approaches
3. **Optional reading:** a16z "How 100 Enterprise CIOs Are Building and Buying Gen AI" + DUNNIXER vendor evaluation scorecard
4. **Optional:** AI+Code Lab Exercises 1--2 + Demos 1--4
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Homework. First, produce one complete deliverable using the XYZ Corp Data Simulation. Not a single question --- a full report. A memo, a comparison, a risk assessment. Whatever interests you. Save or screenshot the output and bring it to Session 5."

"Second, required pre-reading --- about 35 minutes total. Three items: the Morgan Stanley case study, the AIMultiple article on cloud vs. local LLMs, and a short piece on what the AI actually sees when it accesses your data. These prepare you for Session 5, which is entirely about deployment and architecture."

"Optional: the a16z enterprise AI survey and the DUNNIXER vendor evaluation scorecard. Keep the DUNNIXER scorecard --- you will use it in Session 5."

"Also optional: the AI+Code Lab exercises and recorded demos. If you want to build an agent yourself, Exercises 1 and 2 walk you through it with AI assistance."
:::

---

## Session 5 Preview

::: {.highlight-box}
**Session 5: "What Does Deployment Look Like?"**

- Where does the LLM run? Cloud vs. on-premise vs. hybrid
- What does the AI actually see? Data security approaches
- Build vs. buy vs. extend --- the real decision matrix
- Morgan Stanley case study discussion

Come prepared: read the case study and form an opinion.
:::

::: {.notes}
Say: "Next session is about deployment. You have seen what the technology can do. Now the question is: how do you actually put this into production at your company? Where does the LLM run? What data does it see? Do you build, buy, or extend?"

"Come prepared. Read the Morgan Stanley case study and form an opinion --- was their decision the right one? What would your company do differently? See you Monday."
:::

---

## Session 4 Summary {.centered-statement}

## From Data to Deliverable

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
6
:::
::: {.stat-label}
systems queried in one prompt
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
50
:::
::: {.stat-label}
lines of code in the core agent loop
:::
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.stat-number}
90%
:::
::: {.stat-label}
manual prep reduction at HPE
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Recap slide to display as students leave. Key takeaways: the pipeline goes from one prompt to a complete deliverable, the core agent is surprisingly simple (50 lines), and this is already working at scale in companies like HPE.
:::
